summary: Kenneth documents a weekly covered call trading session generating $1,454 profit (4.6% return) on a $31,400 deployed capital across HIMS, OLO, and Oscar Health positions. He addresses two critical risks: forced stock ownership during downturns and capped upside potential, plus taxation implications of short-term capital gains from weekly expiration cycles.
takeaways: Weekly covered calls target ~$180 profit per week with Friday or 2-week expirations
Deploy 100-300 shares per position to generate $300-$500 premium per contract
Accept capped upside (e.g., OLO skyrocketed to $77 but shares called away at $68.88 strike, limiting profit to 4% vs. potential 11%)
Extended expirations (2 weeks vs. 1 week) can generate higher premiums—compare both before selling
Short-term capital gains tax is cost of doing business; set aside ~22% of profits in HYSA to cover tax liability
If stock drops after purchase, can either buy call back and sell stock at loss, or hold and collect premium—requires comfort owning underlying
Risk: Must be willing to own the stock if assigned; if you wouldn't hold the stock long-term, don't sell covered calls on it

strategy_candidates.change: Standardize strike selection rule: only sell calls at strikes where assignment is acceptable, or use 2-3 week expirations when weekly premium is insufficient to compensate for assignment risk
strategy_candidates.evidence: Kenneth notes OLO opportunity cost ($1k upside capped) and mentions choosing 2-week expirations on Oscar Health and HIMS specifically because 'premium was a lot higher'—implies premium-to-risk tradeoff is explicit decision point for Brian's $1,250/mo target

title: Part 8.5 7/26/25 $180 a week: risks and taxes with covered calls
